stem: In a advertising study comparing Platform A vs. Platform B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher conversion rate (64.3% vs 59.2%). However, when stratified by product category: In low-consideration products (n_X=20, n_Y=100): Treatment X achieves 79% vs Treatment Y's 70%. In high-consideration products (n_X=100, n_Y=40): Treatment X achieves 55% vs Treatment Y's 49%. The true population proportion of low-consideration products is uncertain, estimated between 43% and 60%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to high-consideration products (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.073, 0.078], point estimate: 0.076. Treatment X is genuinely better by 7.6 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=59.2%, Y=64.3% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In low-consideration products: X=79% > Y=70%. In high-consideration products: X=55% > Y=49%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Platform A is applied more to high-consideration products (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
